one_line_takeaway: VRP jailbreaks MLLMs by making the image itself role-play a 'high-risk character' plus the verbatim malicious question, adding jailbreak context that raises attack success by ~14% over prior structure-based image attacks while surviving system-prompt and ECSO defenses.
key_finding: On the RedTeam-2K and HarmBench test sets across 5 MLLMs, query-specific VRP achieves the highest ASR of all compared attacks, beating the strongest baselines by an average 14.3% ASR margin (specifically +9.8% over FigStep and +14.3% over Query-Relevant). VRP also generally beats a text-only Textual Role-Play (TRP) variant, indicating the character IMAGE, not just the role-play text, matters. The universal VRP character transfers across black-box models with high ASR, and VRP remains effective under two defenses (a safety system prompt and ECSO), degrading far less than baselines. Combining VRP's role-play template with FigStep or Query-Relevant also boosts those baselines' ASR.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Visual Role-Play (VRP), a structure-based jailbreak attack on Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) that is, by the authors' claim, the first to bring the concept of 'role-play' into MLLM jailbreaks through the visual modality. Instead of merely reformatting a malicious query into an image (as prior structure-based attacks like FigStep and Query-Relevant/MM-SafetyBench do), VRP builds an image that depicts a 'high-risk character' (e.g., 'a cyber criminal with political motivations') and pairs it with a benign role-play instruction that tells the target model to act as the person in the image. The character portrayal supplies the missing 'jailbreak context' that the authors argue simple typography transformations lack. VRP is presented in two settings: a query-specific pipeline (a tailored character per query) and a universal pipeline that optimizes a single reusable character via an LLM-driven search over candidate characters selected by ASR.
threat_model: Black-box attack against MLLMs. The adversary has no access to model parameters, hidden states, or gradients and cannot fine-tune the model; it can only issue single-turn queries (a text-image pair) and read the textual response, subject to any predetermined system prompt. Goal: induce the victim MLLM to answer prohibited/harmful queries with relevant, toxic content rather than refusing. The adversary can use auxiliary open models it controls (an LLM for character description and a text-to-image diffusion model) to construct the attack input offline.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) VRP depends on the victim model having strong instruction-following and image-understanding ability, so it is less effective against weak/poorly-performing MLLMs that cannot play roles; (2) character diffusion prompts are produced by direct LLM generation, so attack quality is bounded by the LLM's prompt-writing ability and by the diffusion model's ability to render the character. Reviewer-observed limitations: absolute ASR values are modest (mostly 24-42%), so a majority of queries still fail; Gemini-Pro-Vision is only evaluated on HarmBench, not RedTeam-2K; the ASR judges (Llama Guard, Mistral-7B) are themselves imperfect classifiers, and the 'relevance' oracle is a customized choice; the tested models are 2024-era and the attack's durability against newer aligned MLLMs is untested; being an offensive paper, no defense is contributed.

datasets_used: RedTeam-2K (2000 harmful textual questions across 16 harmful categories; split 6:2:2 into train/valid/test)
HarmBench (320-question textual harmful test set from the automated red-teaming framework)
quantitative_results: Overall: VRP outperforms strongest baselines by an average 14.3% ASR margin across all models; +9.8% over FigStep, +14.3% over Query-Relevant
Query-specific VRP ASR on RedTeam-2K test set: LLaVA-V1.6-Mixtral 38.00%, Qwen-VL-Chat 29.50%, OmniLMM-12B 28.50%, InternVL-Chat-V1.5 28.25%
Query-specific VRP ASR on HarmBench: LLaVA-V1.6-Mixtral 41.64%, Qwen-VL-Chat 30.28%, OmniLMM-12B 31.55%, InternVL-Chat-V1.5 34.38%, Gemini-Pro-Vision 37.85%

Universal VRP transfer: average ASR 32.7% with LLaVA-V1.6-Mixtral as target model and 29.4% with Qwen-VL-Chat as target; LLaVA target achieves 45.00% ASR on itself and 33.00% transferred to Qwen-VL-Chat (RedTeam-2K)
Ablation (RedTeam-2K): full VRP (I_t2i + I_kt) beats single-component and benign-substituted variants, e.g., LLaVA 38.00% vs 32.75% (I_t2i only), 27.50% (I_kt only), 30.00% (benign character image)
Defense robustness (LLaVA, RedTeam-2K): VRP 38.00% basic -> 30.00% under System Prompt Defense -> 30.50% under ECSO, versus FigStep 15.00->4.25->14.00 and Query-Relevant 20.50->1.75->10.25
Integration: adding VRP template raises FigStep ASR (e.g., LLaVA 15.00->24.50) and Query-Relevant ASR (e.g., Qwen 16.75->30.25) on RedTeam-2K
baselines_compared: Vanilla Typography (typography of raw query as image + generic fulfil instruction)
Textual Role-Play / TRP (same VRP character inserted as text, blank image)
Query-Relevant / MM-SafetyBench (SD+Typo variant, visualizing query keywords)
